coredata.dc:title: Molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on synergistic interaction of honeycomb-like Ni-MOF decorated with AgNPs and N-GQDs for ultra-sensitive detection of olaquindox in animal-origin food
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Shuang
Sun, Ruonan
Zhao, Le
Yan, Chen
Chu, Hongtao
coredata.dc:description: Olaquindox (OLA) in food from its illegal use possesses great harmful effects on humans, making it important to develop sensitive, inexpensive, and convenient methods for OLA detection. This study innovatively presented a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on the synergistic effects of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and a nickel-based metal–organic framework functionalized with silver nanoparticles (Ag/Ni-MOF) for OLA detection. N-GQDs and Ag/Ni-MOF with unique honeycomb structures were sequentially modified on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to accelerate the electron transfer rate and increase the available region of the electrode. Molecularly imprinted polymers were further grown on the Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE by electropolymerization to significantly enhance the selective recognition of OLA. The constructed sensor showed excellent performance for selective OLA determination, with a wide linear range (5–600 nmol·L-1) and exceedingly low detection limit (2.2 nmol·L-1). The sensor was successfully applied to detect OLA in animal-origin food with satisfactory recoveries (96.22–101.02%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular imprinting
Electrochemical sensor
MOF
Olaquindox
N-GQDs
Animal-origin food

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DPV responses of OLA on different modified electrodes
Performance analysis of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22005161), and the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (No: 135409101, 135509108).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the Ni-MOF (A), Ag/Ni-MOF (B), NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (D) MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before removal of OLA (E) and MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (F); elemental mapping of Ag/Ni-MOF (C); TEM images of Ni-MOF (G), Ag/Ni-MOF (H) and N-GQDs (I); FT-IR spectra (J) of Ag/Ni-MOF (a), Ni-MOF (b), and N-GQDs (c); XRD spectra (K) of Ag/Ni-MOF (a), Ni-MOF (b), and N-GQDs (c); XPS survey spectrum (L), Ni2p (M), C1s (N) and Ag3d (O) of Ag/Ni-MOF.

CV (A) and EIS (B) for the bare GCE (a), Ni-MOF/GCE (b), Ag/Ni-MOF/GCE (c), N-GQDs/GCE (d) and Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (e) in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4; CV (C) and EIS (D) for Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (a), MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before elution (b), MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after elution (c), MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE after incubation in 10−7 M OLA solution (d) and NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (e) in 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]-3/-4.
Effects of different conditions: scan rates (A), scan cycles of MIP (B), molar ratio of ANI and OLA (C), pH value of electropolymerization (D), elution time (E) and adsorption time (F).

DPV curves of MIECS in different concentrations of OLA (from a to i): 5, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 nmol·L-1. Inset: the linear relationship between the change value of the peak current and the concentrations of OLA.
Procedure of the preparation of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor.
Determination of OLA in fish muscle, swine muscle and chicken breast samples using the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor and HPLC with the standard addition method. (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on synergistic interaction of honeycomb-like Ni-MOF decorated with AgNPs and N-GQDs for ultra-sensitive detection of olaquindox in animal-origin food

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Catalytic Synthesis for Fine Chemicals, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Heilongjiang Provincial Key Laboratory of Catalytic Synthesis for Fine Chemicals

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar 161006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Olaquindox (OLA) in food from its illegal use possesses great harmful effects on humans, making it important to develop sensitive, inexpensive, and convenient methods for OLA detection. This study innovatively presented a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on the synergistic effects of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and a nickel-based metal–organic framework functionalized with silver nanoparticles (Ag/Ni-MOF) for OLA detection. N-GQDs and Ag/Ni-MOF with unique honeycomb structures were sequentially modified on the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) surface to accelerate the electron transfer rate and increase the available region of the electrode. Molecularly imprinted polymers were further grown on the Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE by electropolymerization to significantly enhance the selective recognition of OLA. The constructed sensor showed excellent performance for selective OLA determination, with a wide linear range (5–600 nmol·L-1) and exceedingly low detection limit (2.2 nmol·L-1). The sensor was successfully applied to detect OLA in animal-origin food with satisfactory recoveries (96.22–101.02%).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Olaquindox (OLA) is a chemically synthesized antibacterial growth promoter. It has been widely used in aquaculture and animal farming to increase feed conversion and treat bacterial illnesses (Wang et al., 2014). However, the genotoxicity, mutagenicity and carcinogenicity of OLA have serious side effects on livestock and humans (Song et al., 2011). To ensure food safety, the European Union and Health Canada have banned the use of OLA as a feed additive (Commission Regulation (EC) No 2788/98, 1998). In China, OLA has been banned for use in poultry and aquaculture (Ministry of Agriculture of the PRC, 2014). However, the illegal use of OLA remains a serious problem due to its economic efficiency in modern agriculture, which has created a great problem for human food safety. Hence, the development of rapid, convenient and sensitive detection methods for OLA in animal-origin food is of great significance.

Some methods for determining OLA have been developed, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Song et al., 2011), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Wang et al., 2014) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry methods (Dibai et al., 2015). However, chromatography requires expensive instruments, complex procedures and skilled professionals. ELISAs are time-consuming and susceptible to environmental influence and require expensive antibodies. In recent years, sensors have been widely developed for clinical diagnosis (Wang et al., 2023; Shen et al., 2023), environmental monitoring (Shao et al., 2022) and food analysis (Wu et al., 2022) due to their dynamic response, resistance to harsh environments and high reliability. For example, Yin et al. (Yin et al., 2022) prepared a simple nanofibrous sensor for fluorescent detection of Fe3+. He et al. (He et al., 2022) prepared a wearable strain/pressure sensor based on carbon nanotube-modified silk nonwoven fabric for real-time health monitoring. Electrochemical sensors (ECSs) have the advantages of simplicity in operation, low cost and immediate detection. However, many modified electrodes have no ability to separate the target from complicated matrices, and the selectivity cannot be guaranteed sufficiently.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) are becoming promising recognition elements in ECSs due to their predetermined selectivity, long-term stability, and resistance to harsh environmental conditions (Han et al., 2022). Molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensors (MIECS) combine the advantages of MIP and ECSs and have shown broad application prospects in environmental analysis (Nguyen et al., 2023), drug detection (Fu et al., 2022), food safety (Wang et al., 2022) and other fields. Currently, researchers have developed MIECS for OLA detection. For example, Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2017) prepared MIECS based on carboxylated multiwalled carbon nanotubes and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs/cMWCNTs) for determining OLA. Bai et al. (Bai et al., 2020) constructed MIECS on a dopamine@graphene (DGr) modified electrode for OLA detection. Despite their selectivity capabilities, MIECS are always restricted by the limited sensing area of the electrode, incomplete removal of templates and high diffusion barriers, which limit the number of recognition sites. The number of imprinted sites that recognize templates greatly influences the sensitivity of MIECS. Thus, using supporting materials with a large surface area can extend the imprinted film and generate more imprinted cavities to effectively improve sensitivity.

Due to their unique high pore structure and enormous internal surface area, metal–organic frameworks (MOF) show excellent potential for applications in the fields of pollutant adsorption (Jia et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2021), drug delivery (Han et al., 2022), energy storage (Gao et al., 2021), catalysis (Xue et al., 2022), and others. Surprisingly, MOF can significantly change and increase the morphology and surface area of MIP films when applied in MIECS (Han et al., 2022). As a kind of MOF with excellent electrocatalytic performance, Ni-MOF can be prepared by different methods to obtain different morphologies, such as columnar, cubic block, lamellar and three-dimensional structures. Therefore, to enhance the number of recognition sites and improve the MIECS performance for OLA detection, we proposed using a Ni-MOF with a three-dimensional structure as the MIECS substrate. This would further increase the available region of the electrode and the number of recognition sites, which greatly promote the accumulation of templates on the material surface. Moreover, the imprinted sites of MIP exposed on the Ni-MOF surface are expected to make the imprinted composites exhibit better binding ability and faster rebinding kinetics. In addition, Ni-MOF with a unique honeycomb three-dimensional structure could effectively reduce the mass transfer resistance and increase the charge transmission rate due to their large specific surface area and ultrashort transmission channel (Gao et al., 2021).

However, in terms of electrochemical analysis, enhancing the conductivity of Ni-MOF is essential. Compared with other metal nanoparticles (NPs), AgNPs have the advantages of a high electron transfer rate, excellent catalytic performance, high antibacterial ability, easy preparation and good biocompatibility (Han et al., 2022) and are widely used as antibacterial materials (Ran et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021), conductive materials (Cheng et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022), and sensing materials (Liu et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2022). Therefore, doping AgNPs in Ni-MOF by simple chemical reduction is expected to solve the problem of poor electrical conductivity of Ni-MOF in this study. Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that can trap electrons in quantum structures and improve sensitivity by facilitating electron transfer to the electrode surface (Algadi et al., 2021). Because of their high charge carrier mobility, excellent electrocatalytic activity and powerful electronic storage capacity, they have displayed important application value in the fields of bioimaging (Chung et al., 2021), chemical sensing (Pathak et al., 2019) and photoelectric catalysis (Ni et al., 2022; Algadi et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2022). There have been some studies on using N-GQDs as electrode modifiers to promote the sensing performance of MIECS. For example, Pathak et al. (Pathak et al., 2019) synthesized MIP on N-GQDs modified screen-printed carbon electrodes. The addition of N-GQDs increased the diffusion coefficient and electron transfer kinetics because of the good catalytic kinetics and high electron mobility of N-GQDs.

Based on the abovementioned factors, for the purpose of developing selective and ultrasensitive OLA detection in food, we designed a novel MIECS based on the synergistic effect of Ni-MOF with a 3D honeycomb flower-sphere structure modified by AgNPs and N-GQDs (MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE). To the best of our knowledge, there has been no report based on the incorporation of Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs and MIP for the electrochemical analysis of OLA until now. The unique flower-shaped Ni-MOF has a favorable aspect ratio and large specific surface area, which promotes the exposure of more binding sites and accelerates the mass transfer rate. Outstanding electrical conductivity will be exhibited by dropping Ag. To further stimulate the electron transfer capability of the electrodes, N-GQDs are introduced. The synergistic effect of Ag/Ni-MOF and N-GQDs is expected to improve the sensitivity of MIECS. Finally, the designed sensor was successfully applied to the detection of OLA in animal-origin food.

Olaquindox (OLA, 98%), quinocetone (QUIN, 98%), mequindox (MEQ, 98%), quinocide (QUI, 98%) and enrofloxacin (ENR, 98%) were purchased from Macklin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Aniline (ANI, ≥99.0%), nickel (II) chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 99%), terephthalic acid (H2BDC, 99%), urea (Ur, 99%), citric acid (CA, 99%) and silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Potassium ferricyanide (K3(Fe(CN)6]), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), methanol and hydrazine hydrate were purchased from Tianjin Reagent Plant (Tianjin, China). All solutions were prepared using deionized water.

N-GQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal methods (Qu et al., 2014). The Ni-MOF was prepared as follows. First, 0.237 g NiCl2·6H2O (1 mmol) and 0.166 g H2BDC (1 mmol) were dissolved in DMF (35 mL). Then, 2.5 mL ethanol and 2.5 mL water were slowly added to the above solution. The mixture was put into an autoclave and kept for 10 h at 160 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the product was washed with DMF, ethanol and water. Finally, the Ni-MOF was dispersed into 2 mL deionized water.

1 mL AgNO3 solution (0.6 mol·L-1) was added to the Ni-MOF solution and stirred for 2 h. The above mixture was then added to 0.2 mL of hydrazine hydrate. The products were separated by centrifugation and washed with water and ethanol. Next, the samples were dried at 60 °C for 25 h to obtain the Ag/Ni-MOF.

All electrochemical measurements were performed on a CHI 660C electrochemical workstation (CH Instruments, Chenhua Co., Shanghai, China) with three electrodes: a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode and a potassium chloride-saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode. The GCE was polished with Al2O3 powders and sonicated in ethanol and aqueous solutions. The dried electrode was activated with 1 mol·L-1 sulfuric acid.

N-GQDs (5 μL, 2.0 mg·mL−1) dispersed in DMF were dripped on the surface of the GCE and dried under an infrared lamp. Ten microliters of Ag/Ni-MOF (5.0 mg·mL−1, dispersed in DMF) suspension was then dripped on the modified electrode and dried to obtain Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE.

The Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE was inserted into 5 mL phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.0) containing 8 mmol·L-1 ANI and 2 mmol·L-1 OLA for electropolymerization by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Twenty cycles were performed at a potential of −0.2 V to + 1.2 V with a scan rate of 100 mV·s−1. Then, the modified electrode was stirred in the eluent of methanol/acetic acid (V/V = 8:2) for 10 min to elute the embedded OLA to obtain the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. The nonimprinted polymer-modified electrode (NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE) was prepared by the above process without the addition of OLA.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out through CV (potential range: −0.2 V to + 0.8 V, scan rate: 100 mV·s−1) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, potential: 0.2 V, frequency range: 100 mHz-100 kHz). The electrode response was then recorded using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), which has a frequency rate, modulation amplitude and step potential of 50 mV·s−1, 25 mV and 5 mV, respectively. The current difference (Δi = i0-i) was considered as the response signal, in which i0 is the peak height of K3[Fe(CN)6] after template removal and i represents the peak height after OLA rebinding.

Fresh animal-origin food (100 g each of fish muscle, swine muscle and chicken breast) purchased from local supermarkets (Qiqihar, China) were selected as the real samples. The authenticity of the real samples as blank samples have been confirmed by the local Food and Drug Inspection and Testing Center responsible for market sampling testing. The samples were stored in a refrigerator at 4 ℃ for use and the standard addition method was implemented.

The real homogenized samples were accurately weighed to 2 g and added to centrifuge tubes containing different concentrations of OLA (11, 22 and 44 nM). The samples were mixed evenly by ultrasonic stirring and placed overnight. The target compounds were then extracted twice using 5 mL of methanol and deionized water-based extraction solution (5:95, v/v). The supernatants of two extracts were collected by centrifugation, mixed and shaken with 2 mL n-hexane to remove fat. The resulting n-hexane layer was removed using disposable pipets. Afterward, the mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and the filtrate was subjected to electrochemical screening.

HPLC was used to validate the results of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE assay. The sample extraction was similar to the above experimental procedure. The obtained supernatant was blown dry with nitrogen and then dissolved in 1.0 mL of 15% aqueous methanol. Afterward, it was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane and the filtrate was subjected to HPLC detection. HPLC analysis was carried out on a Dalian Elite P230 series HPLC system with a UV–vis detector. Chromatographic detection was performed on a C18 column. The mobile phase was a 15% methanol aqueous solution, the detection wavelength was 380 nm, the flow rate was 1.0 mL·min−1, and the injection volume was 10 μL.

The experimental principle is shown in Scheme 1 . The deprotonated H2BDC and Ni2+ form bridged ordered chains by coordination, and the bridged ordered chains further form a 3D honeycomb flower-sphere structure ([(Ni3(OH)2·(C8H4O4)X·(H2O)6-x]·2H2O) by self-assembly. Hydrazine hydrate was used to convert AgNO3 to Ag, and then AgNPs were loaded on the surface of Ni-MOF by electrostatic action and physical adsorption. N-GQDs synthesized by the “top-down” approach and the Ag/Ni-MOF were modified on the GCE surface to enhance the electrochemical signal. Subsequently, the OLA template molecule was electropolymerized with the functional monomer ANI on the Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. Removal of OLA resulted in the formation of imprinted cavities that matched the spatial structure and binding site. The [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-probe experienced a redox reaction on the GCE surface when the OLA concentration varied.

A Hitachi S-4300 electron microscope was used to capture scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the synthesized materials to demonstrate the morphology of the material. Fig. 1 A shows that the prepared Ni-MOF has a honeycomb flower-sphere structure composed of many two-dimensional nanosheets, which is a typical hierarchical structure. The honeycomb-like structure of Ni-MOF is beneficial for electrolytes to diffuse into the interior, speeding up charge transport. Furthermore, the fine layered structure of the Ni-MOF surface can effectively prevent aggregation of AgNPs and reduce the embedding of template molecules. The Ag/Ni-MOF still maintains the unique structure (Fig. 1B), proving that the doping of AgNPs does not affect the morphology of Ni-MOF. We characterized the prepared composites by EDX mapping image analysis (Fig. 1C) to ascertain the elemental distribution. The figures show that O, Ni, and Ag elements are uniformly distributed in the composite, which validates the successful preparation of the Ag/Ni-MOF composite. Fig. 1G shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7650) image of the Ni-MOF, and the thin nanosheet at the edge can be clearly seen, which is consistent with the SEM image. As a support material, it can offer a larger specific surface area for the MIP and thus improve adsorption performance. In addition, in Fig. 1H, AgNPs are modified on the Ni-MOF. As shown in Fig. 1I, the diameters of the N-GQDs are approximately 5 nm, and there is no obvious aggregation, indicating their high dispersion.

The NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before the removal of OLA showed dense and rigid surfaces (Fig. 1D, 1E). However, the surface of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE was rough and porous (Fig. 1F) because removal of the template molecule left imprinted cavities on the electrode surface.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, NEXUS 470, USA) was further applied to confirm the functional group information of the composites as shown in Fig. 1J (curves a, b). The peaks at 1620 cm−1 and 3440 cm−1 are attributed to the bending and stretching vibrations of the OH groups (Jiang et al., 2022). The peaks at 1580 cm−1 and 1360 cm−1 are asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the –COO- groups of p-phthalic acid. The peak at 720 cm−1 is attributed to the bending vibration of the Ni-O bond (Zhang et al., 2021). The above characteristic peaks indicate that Ni-MOF was successfully prepared. Moreover, the infrared spectrum of Ag/Ni-MOF is consistent with that of Ni-MOF, suggesting that the doping of AgNPs does not change the functional groups of Ni-MOF. For the FT-IR spectrum of N-GQDs (curve c), the peaks at 3430 cm−1 and 3220 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of –NH and –OH groups, respectively. The peaks at 1670 cm−1 and 1590 cm−1 are stretching vibrations of CO and CC. The peaks at 1410 cm−1 and 1230 cm−1 are attributed to the in-plane bending vibration of OH and the stretching vibration of CO, respectively. The results show that N-containing groups have been successfully introduced into the skeleton of N-GQDs. These functional groups could enhance the stability of N-GQDs in solution.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) was performed using a German Bruker D8-FOCUS to determine the crystalline structures of the Ag/Ni-MOF (curve a), Ni-MOF (curve b) and N-GQDs (curve c), as shown in Fig. 1K. In the XRD analysis of the Ni-MOF, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 8.5°, 17.0° and 28.1° correspond to the crystal planes (100), (101) and (020) of the Ni-MOF (CCDCNo.638866), respectively. For Ag/Ni-MOF, in addition to the diffraction peaks of the Ni-MOF, the diffraction peaks at 33.8°, 46.2°, 61.4° and 77.3° correspond to the crystal planes (111), (200), (220), and (311) of Ag (JCPDS No.087–0597). In the XRD pattern of N-GQDs (Fig. 1K), the wide peak at 2θ = 26° is consistent with the (002) diffraction plane of carbon material, which proves the amorphous structure of N-GQDs.

We further characterized the chemical elements and surface composition of the Ag/Ni-MOF by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI, USA). As shown in Fig. 1L, the Ag/Ni-MOF contains Ni, C, O and Ag, which is consistent with the structural composition of Ag/Ni-MOF. In the Ni 2p spectrum (Fig. 1M), the two characteristic peaks located at 872.9 eV and 855.8 eV are Ni 2p1/2 and Ni 2p3/2 of Ni2+, and the peaks located at 860.5 eV and 879.8 eV are the satellite peaks of Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1N) has two peaks at 285.1 and 289.2 eV from CC and CO bonds. In Fig. 1O, the peaks at binding energies of 366.9 eV and 374.1 eV correspond to Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 of Ag2+, respectively. These results further verified that the Ag/Ni-MOF composites were successfully prepared.

CV and EIS were used to determine the electrochemical behavior of different modified electrodes. In Fig. 2 A, compared with the bare electrode (curve a) and Ni-MOF/GCE (curve b), the peak current of Ag/Ni-MOF/GCE (curve c) rose significantly, which could be ascribed to the excellent electron transport capability of AgNPs (Wu et al., 2022). The response current of N-GQDs/GCE (curve d) was enhanced compared with that of GCE (curve a) because of the high mobility of the electric load flow and the excellent electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs (Hatamluyi et al., 2020). However, Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE had the largest peak current (curve e). This indicated that the synergistic effect between the materials further improved the electrode performance. Fig. 2C shows that the current of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE before the removal of OLA (curve b) was obviously weaker than that of Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (curve a), which suggested that the nonconducting MIP film covered the electrode surface. After elution of OLA (curve c), specific cavities for selective stereochemical recognition of OLA were produced on the surface of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, which enhanced the porosity and diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. Thus, the peak current increased, verifying the efficient removal of OLA from the MIP layer. When MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE rebounded to OLA (curve d), the peak current markedly decreased as the imprinted cavities were refilled, which inhibited the diffusion of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-. A dense film on the surface of NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (curve e) led to the blockage of electron transfer, so the current was weak. According to these findings, the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE could be used as a valuable indicator for potential practical application in OLA detection.

The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the Ag/Ni-MOF/GCE (curve c) was significantly reduced compared with that of the GCE (curve a) in Fig. 2B because the Ag/Ni-MOF could promote the electron mobility of the electrode and improve its conductivity. The Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (curve e) had the smallest semicircle diameter due to the high electrocatalytic activity of N-GQDs. Meanwhile, nitrogen doping enhances the recombination of the electron-hole pairs induced by the amino functional groups, which can further improve the electrical conductivity of N-GQDs (Chung et al., 2021). In Fig. 2D, the radius of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor (curve c) diminished compared with that of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor before the removal of OLA (curve b). This might be due to the cavities on the imprinted layer that facilitated the attachment of the probe to the electrode surface. In addition, the Rct of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE increased significantly after rebinding to OLA (curve d). These results supported the findings of the CV study and indicated the good conductivity and large effective surface area of Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs.

The effect of the scan rate on the peak current was investigated. Fig. S1 shows a satisfactory linear relationship between the peak current and the square root of the scanning speed. The regression equation is Ipa = 224.94ν1/2 + 104.30, R2 = 0.995; Ipc = -231.11ν1/2-49.63, R2 = 0.998, which indicated a diffusion-controlled process for reaction on the electrode surface (Hatamluyi et al., 2020).

To verify the improvement in the electrochemical performance of Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, the Randles-Sevcik equation (Eq. (1) was used to evaluate the electroactive surface area of the different electrodes.
(1)
I p
= 268600 ·
n
3 / 2
· A ·
D
1 / 2
· C ·
V
1 / 2
here, Ip is the peak anodizing current (A), the quantity of electrons transferred is n (n = 1), A represents the electroactive surface area (cm2), the diffusion coefficient of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- is denoted as D (0.67 × 10-5 cm2·s−1), C is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (5 × 10-3 mol·cm−3), and V1/2 represents the square root of the scan rate (v·s−1) (Wang et al., 2022). The bare electrode and Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE have surface areas of 0.054 cm−2 and 0.198 cm−2, respectively. The findings revealed that Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs significantly increased the electroactive surface area. The larger effective surface area of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE could promote the adsorption capacity to OLA and thus improve the sensitivity of the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPV responses of OLA on different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with other methods, the DPV method has better current sensitivity and resolution (Bai et al., 2020), so the current response of DPV with different modified electrodes in 100 nM OLA solution was measured (Fig. S2). The response peak of DPV is attributed to the redox of NO bonds in OLA (Wang et al., 2019). The increase in current on MIP/Ni-MOF/GCE may be ascribed to Ni-MOF providing a large surface area to locate more imprinted sites. Due to the high conductivity of AgNPs, the peak current of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/GCE was further enhanced. N-GQDs can capture electrons due to the quantum confinement effect, which makes the sensors have lower electron transfer resistance. In addition to their respective advantages, the synergistic effect between Ag/Ni-MOF and N-GQDs also contributed to the sensing performance of the MIECS. In particular, N-GQDs possess many anchoring sites for metal ions (Liu et al., 2022), and their integration with Ag/Ni-MOF separates charges generated at the interface. In contrast, the response peaks of NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE to OLA almost disappeared. This was caused by the poor conductivity of NIP and the absence of specific recognition sites on Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. The above findings suggest that the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE has significantly better responsiveness and higher sensitivity in OLA detection than the other sensors. The advantage is attributed to the excellent electron transport capability of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE and more specific recognition sites.

The scan rate has an important effect on the density of the MIP film during electropolymerization. The low scan rate will result in a loose MIP film, reducing the number of recognition sites. However, a highly dense MIP film will inhibit the elution and readsorption of template molecules. Fig. 3 A shows that when the scan rate was 100 mV·s−1, the amount of imprinted cavities was the largest and the ΔI value, namely the current difference of the sensors before and after rebinding 10−7 M OLA, was the greatest. The number of electropolymerization cycles determines the thickness of the MIP film. If there are few cycles of polymerization, the imprinting film formed will be easily damaged. However, the thicker polymer film will embed the template in the membrane. In this study, when the number of polymerization cycles was 20, the thickness of the imprinting film suited the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE (Fig. 3B).

An excess of functional monomer will lead to self-polymerization, and inadequate monomer cannot combine with sufficient template molecules to produce enough recognition sites, reducing the sensitivity of MIECS. Fig. 3C shows that the ΔI value was the highest when the molar ratio of OLA to ANI was 1:4. The pH value of the solution during polymerization and adsorption not only significantly affects the electron transport rate in the solution but also affects the hydrogen bond between template molecules and functional monomers. According to Fig. 3D, when the pH value was 7, ΔI reached the maximum, indicating that under this condition, the hydrogen bond force between OLA and ANI was the strongest.

In Fig. 3E, the ΔI value increased with increasing of elution time, indicating more and more imprinted cavities were formed. After 10 min, ΔI reached the maximum value and then decreased dramatically. The reason for this phenomenon may be the collapse of the 3D imprinted structure in the imprinting film due to excessive elution time, which would prevent electron transfer. Therefore, the optimal elution time was chosen as 10 min. The rapid removal of template molecules is because the Ag/Ni-MOF had a honeycomb-like structure and the imprinted sites were exposed on their surface. In Fig. 3F, the current decreased rapidly after 2 min because OLA occupied the cavities exposed on the surface of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs in a short time. After 10 min, the current was relatively unchanged. Thus, 10 min was determined as the optimized response time for the detection of OLA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations (5–600 nmol·L-1) of OLA were detected by the DPV method to evaluate the analytical performance of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE. In Fig. 4 , the peak anode current of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- decreased as the OLA concentration increased. With the increase in OLA rebinding, more imprinted cavities were occupied, which impeded the electron transfer of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. In the inset, when the OLA concentration was in the range of 5–600 nmol·L-1, the change in the peak current (ΔI) was proportional to the OLA concentration (c). The linear regression equation is ΔI(μA) = 0.021c (nmol·L-1) + 3.80 (R2 = 0.996) and the sensitivity is 0.021 μA·nM−1. The LOD calculated according to the formula LOD = 3 s/S was 2.2 nmol·L-1, where s is the standard deviation of the detector response for the blank sample (s = 0.0155 in this experiment, three repeated measurements) and S is the sensitivity of the electrode (Nguyen et al., 2023). The MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor was compared with the reported detection methods for OLA in Table S1. It was found that the sensor constructed in this study has a linear range similar to that of other MIECS. In addition, the LOD of the developed method is lower than that of recently reported methods. This can be ascribed to the synergistic effect of Ag/Ni-MOF and N-GQDs.

Some substances that may coexist (quinocide and enrofloxacin) or have similar structures (quinocetone, mequindox) were selected as interfering substances for selectivity experiments. The DPV response curves of 1 × 10-7 mol·L-1 OLA and OLA in the presence of 5-fold interferences were measured. The DPV response of MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE to OLA was 12.14, 11.00, 13.01 and 12.67 times that of QUIN, MEQ, QUI and ENR, respectively. MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE showed the strongest DPV response to OLA with a current of 58.3 µA in Fig. S3, owing to the complementarity of functional groups, size and shape of imprinted cavities with OLA. Because there is no unique OLA recognition site in the NIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE, the I values of all interfering compounds are incredibly similar to those of the template. The imprinting factor (IF) (IF = IMIP/INIP) is an important parameter in the selectivity study of MIP sensors. The IF value of OLA was 12.51. The recovery of each sample for OLA was above 88.52% in the presence of interfering substances, as shown in Fig. S4. This result indicated that the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor could preferentially identify OLA in the mixture. These results are sufficient to suggest the excellent selectivity and anti-interference ability of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor to OLA, which is conducive to the selective detection of OLA in complicated animal-origin food.

Seven MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensors were independently prepared under the same conditions for 1 × 10-7 mol·L-1 OLA detection as described in Section 2.4. The recoveries were in the range of 96.41%-98.29%. The relative standard deviation (RSD) is 2.55% (Fig. S5A), demonstrating excellent reproducibility of the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor, which shows its great potential for practical applications.

The stability of the sensor was studied by measuring the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE kept in centrifuge tubes at room temperature for 15 days (Fig. S5B). Using the same electrode, 1 × 10-7 mol·L-1 OLA was assayed at three-day intervals. The recoveries were in the range of 94.60%-98.47%. The current values remained at 93.70% of the original values. These results indicated that the sensor had favorable stability and could be applied for long-term OLA detection.

The MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor was applied for OLA determination in fish muscle, swine muscle and chicken breast using the conventional addition method. The recoveries of OLA calculated by the standard addition method ranged from 96.22% to 101.02%, and the RSD ranged from 1.52% to 2.73% as shown in Table 1 . Therefore, the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor shows great potential for portable field electrochemical OLA detection. In addition, HPLC was used to verify the accuracy of the constructed sensor. The analysis results were in good agreement with HPLC, indicating that the sensor has satisfactory accuracy and reliability and could be applied for OLA detection in food samples.

This study reported the development and application of a novel MIECS based on the synergistic effect of N-GQDs and Ag/Ni-MOF for the rapid, sensitive and specific detection of OLA. A unique honeycomb-like structure of Ag/Ni-MOF as a substrate for the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor increased the number of specific recognition sites. Under optimized conditions, the MIP/Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs/GCE sensor presented superior sensing capability toward OLA in terms of a wide linear range (5–600 nmol·L-1), and the detection limit was as low as 2.2 nmol·L-1 (S/N = 3). The sensor also presented a low-cost effect, good selectivity, satisfactory stability and reproducibility and was successfully used to conveniently detect OLA in animal-origin foods with favorable recoveries (96.22% to 101.02%). The results were confirmed by HPLC. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that Ag/Ni-MOF/N-GQDs and MIP have been combined for the electrochemical analysis of OLA, providing a new strategy for detecting OLA in food samples.

Shuang Han: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ruonan Sun: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Le Zhao: Validation. Chen Yan: Methodology. Hongtao Chu: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22005161), and the Fundamental Research Funds in Heilongjiang Provincial Universities (No: 135409101, 135509108).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.136001.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
